content.problemStatement: Given is a `N x M` binary matrix, for each cell find its distance from the nearest `0`.
constraints: At least one 0 exists in each test case
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T (1 ≤ T ≤ 10), the number of test cases. For each test case, the first line contains two integers N and M (1 ≤ N, M ≤ 1000) – the number of rows and columns of the binary matrix. The next N lines each contain M space‑separated integers (0 or 1) describing the matrix.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output N lines, each containing M space‑separated integers. The integer at position (i, j) is the minimum Manhattan distance from cell (i, j) to any cell containing 0 in the same test case.

sampleTestCases.explanation: The distance of each cell from the nearest 0 is calculated using BFS.
All cells are 0, so the distance is 0.
There is only one cell, which is 0, so the distance is 0.

solution.approach: Treat every cell containing 0 as a source and run a multi‑source Breadth‑First Search. All 0‑cells are initially enqueued with distance 0. While the queue is not empty, pop a cell, examine its four neighbours (up, down, left, right) and, if a neighbour has not been visited yet, set its distance to current distance + 1 and enqueue it. Because BFS expands level by level, the first time a cell is visited we have found its shortest distance to any 0. This runs in O(N·M) time per test case and uses O(N·M) additional space for the queue and distance matrix.
solution.timeComplexity: O(N·M) per test case – each cell is enqueued and processed at most once.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(N·M) – the queue may hold all cells in the worst case and the matrix itself stores the distances.
